compounds.realWorldUse: Drinking, cooling, cleaning everything
compounds.funFact: Water is the only substance on Earth that naturally exists as solid, liquid and gas. Your body is about 60% water!

compounds.realWorldUse: Fizzy drinks, fire extinguishers, dry ice
compounds.funFact: CO₂ is what makes fizzy drinks bubbly. In high concentrations it can make you pass out — that's why it's used in fire extinguishers!

compounds.realWorldUse: Food seasoning, preserving food, de-icing roads
compounds.funFact: Salt is made from two poisonous substances — sodium metal and chlorine gas — but together they make something safe to eat. Chemistry is wild!

compounds.realWorldUse: Breathing, hospitals, rocket fuel
compounds.funFact: Pure oxygen makes things burn much faster — even materials that don't normally burn in air will burst into flames in pure oxygen!

compounds.realWorldUse: Rocket fuel, fuel cells, making ammonia
compounds.funFact: Hydrogen is the lightest element in the universe and the most abundant. The Hindenburg airship disaster happened because it was filled with hydrogen gas!

compounds.realWorldUse: Fertilisers, cleaning products, refrigeration
compounds.funFact: Ammonia smells absolutely horrible — like very strong wee! That's because your body makes small amounts of it when breaking down protein.

compounds.realWorldUse: Natural gas for cooking and heating
compounds.funFact: Cows produce huge amounts of methane when they burp and fart. Farming contributes significantly to climate change because of this!

compounds.realWorldUse: Bleaching hair, disinfecting wounds, rocket propellant
compounds.funFact: Concentrated hydrogen peroxide is used as rocket fuel! The diluted version from the chemist is only 3% — the rocket stuff is 90%+.

compounds.realWorldUse: Water purification (tiny amounts), making PVC plastic
compounds.funFact: Chlorine gas was used as a chemical weapon in World War 1. Even tiny amounts can damage your lungs — it's extremely dangerous!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — chlorine gas is a deadly poison used as a weapon in WW1!

compounds.realWorldUse: Your stomach makes it to digest food! Also used in industry
compounds.funFact: Your stomach contains hydrochloric acid strong enough to dissolve metal. Your stomach lining replaces itself every few days to survive!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — concentrated HCl will burn through skin and metal!

compounds.realWorldUse: Food packaging to keep things fresh, liquid nitrogen for freezing
compounds.funFact: 78% of the air you breathe is nitrogen! Liquid nitrogen is so cold (-196°C) it instantly freezes anything it touches.

compounds.realWorldUse: Made during incomplete burning — it's a byproduct, not useful!
compounds.funFact: Carbon monoxide is called the silent killer because it has no smell or colour. It kills by stopping your blood from carrying oxygen.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — carbon monoxide is odourless and kills silently. It's extremely dangerous!

compounds.realWorldUse: Found in volcanoes and swamps — not useful, just deadly
compounds.funFact: H₂S smells exactly like rotten eggs. At high concentrations it paralyses your sense of smell so you can't detect it — then it kills you!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — hydrogen sulfide smells like rotten eggs and is extremely toxic!

compounds.realWorldUse: Food preservative (in tiny amounts), making sulfuric acid
compounds.funFact: Volcanoes release huge amounts of SO₂. Large volcanic eruptions can cool the entire planet by blocking sunlight with sulfur clouds!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — SO₂ causes acid rain and damages your lungs!

compounds.realWorldUse: Pigment in paint (Mars red), thermite reactions
compounds.funFact: Mars looks red because its surface is covered in iron oxide — rust! The entire planet is basically one giant rusty rock.

compounds.realWorldUse: Building material, chalk, cement, antacid tablets
compounds.funFact: The White Cliffs of Dover are made of calcium carbonate — billions of tiny shells from sea creatures that lived 70 million years ago!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making cement, treating soil, water purification
compounds.funFact: When you add water to quicklime it gets hot enough to boil the water! Ancient Romans used this reaction as an incendiary weapon.

compounds.realWorldUse: Glass, computer chips, beach sand, quartz watches
compounds.funFact: The glass in your windows, the sand on the beach, and the chips in your computer are all made from silicon dioxide. It's everywhere!

compounds.realWorldUse: Antacid tablets, fireproofing, making cement
compounds.funFact: When magnesium burns it produces blinding white light and magnesium oxide. Photographers used burning magnesium as a flash before electric lights!

compounds.realWorldUse: Rubies and sapphires are aluminium oxide with trace impurities!
compounds.funFact: Rubies and sapphires are the same compound — aluminium oxide — just with different impurities giving them their colour. Gemstones are just fancy chemicals!

compounds.realWorldUse: Anaesthetic, whipped cream dispensers, racing car fuel boost
compounds.funFact: Laughing gas (N₂O) makes people giggle uncontrollably and feel happy. It's also used to make whipped cream and boost car engine power!

compounds.realWorldUse: Makes smog, used in making nitric acid
compounds.funFact: NO₂ is the brown smog you see over cities. Diesel engines produce it, which is why cities are trying to ban diesel cars!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — nitrogen dioxide is a toxic brown gas that causes acid rain and lung damage!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making Teflon (non-stick pans), toothpaste fluoride
compounds.funFact: Fluorine is so reactive it will burn through glass, concrete and even water. Scientists who first tried to isolate it were killed or blinded trying!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — fluorine is the most reactive element on Earth and will react violently with almost everything including water!

compounds.realWorldUse: Etching glass, making semiconductors for phones and computers
compounds.funFact: HF is terrifying — it passes through skin without pain then dissolves your bones from the inside. Even small amounts on skin can be fatal!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — hydrofluoric acid passes through skin painlessly then dissolves your bones. Extremely deadly!

compounds.realWorldUse: Ozone layer protects us from UV rays, used to purify water
compounds.funFact: The ozone layer is just oxygen atoms arranged in groups of three instead of two. Without it, UV radiation from the Sun would make life on Earth impossible!

compounds.realWorldUse: Used as a fumigant to kill pests in stored grain
compounds.funFact: Phosphine was discovered in Venus's atmosphere in 2020, causing excitement about possible alien life! It's also what causes 'will-o'-the-wisps' in marshes.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — phosphine is an extremely toxic gas that can ignite spontaneously in air!

compounds.realWorldUse: Fool's gold is iron sulfide (pyrite FeS₂)!
compounds.funFact: Fool's Gold (iron pyrite) famously tricked gold prospectors during the Gold Rush. It's actually iron sulfide and worth almost nothing!

compounds.realWorldUse: Killing algae in swimming pools, fungicide for crops
compounds.funFact: Copper sulfate crystals are a stunning bright blue. Growing copper sulfate crystals is a classic school chemistry experiment!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making soap, unblocking drains, making paper
compounds.funFact: Soap is made by reacting fats with sodium hydroxide (lye). The lye itself is extremely caustic but the reaction turns it into something gentle enough to wash with!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — sodium hydroxide dissolves skin and causes severe chemical burns!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making lenses for telescopes and cameras, source of fluorine
compounds.funFact: Fluorite glows bright blue under UV light — the word 'fluorescence' was named after this mineral! Some specimens are spectacularly colourful.

compounds.realWorldUse: Baking, antacid, fire extinguishers, cleaning
compounds.funFact: Baking soda and vinegar react to make CO₂, which is why it makes cakes rise! This same reaction powers model volcano science experiments.

compounds.realWorldUse: Colouring glass ruby red, electronics
compounds.funFact: Gold oxide is actually unstable and decomposes back to pure gold when heated — gold is so unreactive it doesn't even like being an oxide!

compounds.realWorldUse: Button batteries in watches, antimicrobial coatings
compounds.funFact: Silver oxide batteries power most small electronics like hearing aids and watches. Silver has natural antimicrobial properties — that's why we used silver cutlery!

compounds.realWorldUse: Sunscreen, nappy cream, white paint, rubber making
compounds.funFact: Zinc oxide is the white stuff lifeguards put on their noses! It blocks UV rays completely, which is why it's in the best sunscreens.

compounds.realWorldUse: White paint, sunscreen, food colouring E171
compounds.funFact: Almost all white paint contains titanium dioxide. It's also in most white sweets and icing sugar to make them look bright white. You've definitely eaten it!

compounds.realWorldUse: Pigment in ceramics and glass, making copper metal
compounds.funFact: The Statue of Liberty is green because copper reacts with air and water to form copper compounds. When new, it was shiny brown like a copper coin!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making fertilisers and explosives like TNT
compounds.funFact: Nitric acid turns skin bright yellow — this is a chemical reaction with proteins. It's used to make most explosives and fertilisers!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — nitric acid causes severe burns and turns your skin bright yellow on contact!

compounds.realWorldUse: Car batteries, making fertilisers, the most produced chemical in industry
compounds.funFact: Sulfuric acid is the most industrially produced chemical in the world. It violently dehydrates organic matter — it can turn sugar to carbon before your eyes!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — sulfuric acid causes catastrophic burns, boils water on contact and can dissolve metals!

compounds.realWorldUse: Drying agent, making phosphoric acid for cola drinks
compounds.funFact: P₂O₅ is one of the most powerful drying agents known — it reacts so violently with water it can extract moisture from other chemicals!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — reacts explosively with water and causes severe burns!

compounds.realWorldUse: Hydrogen bombs, hydrogen source for spacecraft
compounds.funFact: Lithium hydride is used in thermonuclear (hydrogen) bombs! It reacts with neutrons to produce tritium, which fuels the fusion reaction.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — lithium hydride explodes violently with water and is used in nuclear weapons!

compounds.realWorldUse: Weedkiller, making chlorine dioxide for paper bleaching
compounds.funFact: Sodium chlorate is a powerful oxidiser. Mixed with sugar it makes a dangerous explosive — it's been used in improvised bombs!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — sodium chlorate is a powerful explosive oxidiser that can detonate when mixed with organic materials!

compounds.realWorldUse: Water purification, treating fungal infections, chemical synthesis
compounds.funFact: Potassium permanganate makes a stunning purple-pink colour in water. A single crystal dropped into water creates a beautiful swirling purple cloud!

compounds.realWorldUse: Alcoholic drinks, hand sanitiser, fuel, solvent
compounds.funFact: Ethanol is the alcohol in beer, wine and spirits. It's also in hand sanitiser and can run car engines. It burns with an almost invisible blue flame!

compounds.realWorldUse: Welding and cutting metal, making plastics
compounds.funFact: Acetylene burns hotter than almost any other fuel — the oxyacetylene torch reaches 3500°C, hot enough to cut through steel like butter!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — acetylene can explode spontaneously under pressure and burns at 3500°C!

compounds.realWorldUse: Preserving biological specimens, making resins and plastics
compounds.funFact: Formaldehyde is used to preserve dead bodies and specimens in jars. It's in some household products like furniture and can off-gas into your home!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — formaldehyde is a toxic carcinogen that causes cancer and severe respiratory damage!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making acetylene gas for welding
compounds.funFact: Drop calcium carbide in water and it makes acetylene gas — Victorian miners used this reaction in carbide lamps to light coal mines!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — reacts with water to produce explosive acetylene gas!

compounds.realWorldUse: Gunpowder (with charcoal and sulfur), food preservative, fertiliser
compounds.funFact: Saltpetre is one of the three ingredients in gunpowder, known since ancient China. It was so valuable in the Middle Ages that people collected it from dunghills!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — potassium nitrate is a powerful oxidiser and component of gunpowder. It can cause fires and explosions!

compounds.realWorldUse: Used historically to make pure mercury and oxygen
compounds.funFact: Heating mercury oxide decomposes it into liquid mercury and oxygen gas. This is how oxygen was first discovered by Joseph Priestley in 1774!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — mercury is an extremely toxic heavy metal that damages the brain and nervous system!

compounds.realWorldUse: Etching circuit boards, water treatment, photography
compounds.funFact: Iron chloride is used to etch the copper circuit boards inside every electronic device. Without it, we couldn't make smartphones or computers!

compounds.realWorldUse: Salt substitute, fertiliser, lethal injection (controversially)
compounds.funFact: Potassium chloride is used as a low-sodium salt substitute. In very high doses it stops the heart — which is why it's controversially used in lethal injections.

compounds.realWorldUse: De-icing roads, food firming agent (in olives and pickles), drying agent
compounds.funFact: Calcium chloride absorbs so much water from the air it can actually turn into a liquid puddle just sitting out! It melts ice at -30°C.

compounds.realWorldUse: Making special glasses and ceramics for telescopes
compounds.funFact: Lithium compounds are used to treat bipolar disorder. Lithium batteries in your phone and laptop contain lithium compounds that store electrical energy.

compounds.realWorldUse: Food additive, de-icing, making tofu firm
compounds.funFact: Magnesium chloride is what makes tofu firm! It's also found in seawater — when seawater evaporates it leaves behind this and other salts.

compounds.realWorldUse: Medical X-ray contrast agent, white paint pigment
compounds.funFact: Patients drink a 'barium meal' of barium sulfate before X-rays — it shows up bright white and lets doctors see inside your digestive system. It's completely harmless!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making glass, detergents, paper
compounds.funFact: Sodium sulfate (Glauber's salt) was the first chemical produced industrially in large quantities. It was used as a medicine in the 1600s as a powerful laxative!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making antiperspirant deodorant, chemical reactions catalyst
compounds.funFact: Aluminium chloride is the active ingredient in strong antiperspirant deodorants — it blocks sweat glands. It reacts violently with water, steaming and hissing!

compounds.realWorldUse: Green pigment in paint, polishing compound, making chromium metal
compounds.funFact: Chromium oxide is what gives emeralds their green colour! The same compound is used to polish metals to a mirror finish — it's in green polishing compounds.

compounds.realWorldUse: Inside batteries, making glass, bleaching
compounds.funFact: Manganese dioxide is inside most disposable batteries! When hydrogen peroxide touches it, MnO₂ acts as a catalyst making the peroxide rapidly decompose into water and oxygen.

compounds.realWorldUse: Making lead crystal glass, car batteries
compounds.funFact: Ancient Romans used lead compounds as a sweetener in wine and food — this may have contributed to the fall of the Roman Empire through mass lead poisoning!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — lead compounds are cumulative neurotoxins that cause brain damage, especially in children!

compounds.realWorldUse: Nuclear reactor fuel — powers about 10% of the world's electricity
compounds.funFact: One kilogram of uranium fuel produces as much energy as 45,000 kilograms of coal! Nuclear fuel pellets are the size of a fingertip but contain enormous energy.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — uranium dioxide is radioactive and chemically toxic, causing radiation sickness and organ damage!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making liquid soap, batteries, food processing
compounds.funFact: Potassium hydroxide is used to make soft liquid soaps while sodium hydroxide makes hard bar soaps. Both are extremely caustic until they react to form soap!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — potassium hydroxide causes severe chemical burns and destroys tissue on contact!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making cement and plaster, treating acidic soil, water purification
compounds.funFact: Breathe CO₂ into limewater (calcium hydroxide solution) and it goes milky white — this is the classic test for carbon dioxide in school science!

compounds.realWorldUse: Iron supplement tablets, water purification, pigment
compounds.funFact: Ferric hydroxide is a rusty orange colour and forms when iron rusts in water. Your body uses a similar compound to store iron — it's in iron supplement tablets!

compounds.realWorldUse: Psychiatric medicine for bipolar disorder, making lithium batteries
compounds.funFact: Lithium carbonate has been used for centuries — the ancient Greeks drank lithium-rich spring water to treat mental illness without knowing why it worked!

compounds.realWorldUse: Soldering flux, fertiliser, cough medicine, salty liquorice!
compounds.funFact: Ammonium chloride is what makes Scandinavian salty liquorice taste intensely salty and slightly weird. It's also used to help solder metal joints!

compounds.realWorldUse: Table salt additive to prevent iodine deficiency, nuclear emergency tablets
compounds.funFact: After nuclear accidents, people take potassium iodide tablets to protect their thyroid gland from radioactive iodine. It floods the thyroid so it can't absorb the bad stuff!

compounds.realWorldUse: Medical imaging, iodine supplement, photography
compounds.funFact: Sodium iodide crystals are used in radiation detectors — they flash with visible light when hit by gamma rays, allowing scientists to detect radiation!

compounds.realWorldUse: Iron supplements, lawn treatments, making ink
compounds.funFact: Medieval monks made iron gall ink from ferrous sulfate and oak galls to write manuscripts. The Magna Carta was written in iron gall ink!

compounds.realWorldUse: Fireworks (green colour!), catalyst, wood preservative
compounds.funFact: Copper chloride makes beautiful green flames in fireworks! Different copper compounds make different shades — it's a staple of professional pyrotechnics.

compounds.realWorldUse: Fireworks (bright green colour), water softening, rat poison
compounds.funFact: Barium chloride makes brilliant apple-green flames in fireworks. Despite being used in pyrotechnics, it's also used as rat poison — very toxic!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — barium chloride is extremely toxic and has been used as rat poison!

compounds.realWorldUse: Red fireworks and flares, sensitive toothpaste
compounds.funFact: Strontium chloride makes brilliant crimson-red fireworks! It's also in sensitive toothpaste because strontium ions block the tiny tubules in sensitive teeth.

compounds.realWorldUse: Bath salts for sore muscles, growing vegetables, medicine
compounds.funFact: Epsom salts were discovered in Epsom, Surrey in 1618 when a farmer noticed his cows wouldn't drink from a bitter pool — but wounds healed faster when bathed in it!

compounds.realWorldUse: Plaster of Paris, plasterboard walls, food additive in tofu and beer
compounds.funFact: Plaster of Paris is calcium sulfate that absorbs water and sets hard. It's named after gypsum deposits in Paris. Your walls are probably lined with it!

compounds.realWorldUse: Fertiliser, food preservative in cured meats, making explosives
compounds.funFact: Sodium nitrate (Chilean saltpetre) was so valuable in the 19th century that Chile and Bolivia fought a war over it! It's still used to preserve bacon and salami.

compounds.realWorldUse: Fertiliser (most commonly), ANFO mining explosive
compounds.funFact: The 2020 Beirut explosion was caused by 2,750 tonnes of ammonium nitrate stored in a warehouse. It's the most powerful non-nuclear explosion in recent history!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — ammonium nitrate caused the catastrophic 2020 Beirut explosion. It is an extremely powerful explosive!

compounds.realWorldUse: Flame retardants, water treatment, making medicines
compounds.funFact: Bromine is one of only two elements that is liquid at room temperature (the other is mercury). It's dark red and evaporates into toxic orange-brown vapour!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — bromine vapour is severely toxic and causes chemical burns to skin, eyes and lungs!

compounds.realWorldUse: Antiseptic, thyroid medicine, water purification tablets
compounds.funFact: Iodine turns starch a deep blue-black colour — this is the test for starch in biology! It changes colour because iodine molecules get trapped inside the starch spiral.

compounds.realWorldUse: Household bleach, water purification, killing bacteria in swimming pools
compounds.funFact: Never mix bleach with ammonia or vinegar — it creates toxic chloramine gas! This is one of the most common accidental poisonings from household chemicals.

compounds.realWorldUse: Nail polish remover, paint thinner, dissolving plastics
compounds.funFact: Your body naturally produces acetone when burning fat — people on ketogenic diets have detectable acetone in their breath! It's also highly flammable nail polish remover.

compounds.realWorldUse: Safety matches, fireworks, oxygen generation
compounds.funFact: The red tips of safety matches contain potassium chlorate. When heated, it releases pure oxygen which ignites the match. Mixing it with sugar makes a dangerous explosive!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — potassium chlorate mixed with any fuel becomes a shock-sensitive explosive used in bombs!

compounds.realWorldUse: Car airbags — it explodes to rapidly inflate them!
compounds.funFact: Your car's airbag works by exploding sodium azide! The explosion happens in milliseconds, inflating the bag before your face hits the steering wheel.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — sodium azide is a deadly poison AND explosive. Even tiny amounts are fatal!

compounds.realWorldUse: Rocket fuel oxidiser — used in spacecraft and missiles
compounds.funFact: N₂O₄ is used as rocket fuel in spacecraft like the Apollo lunar module! It ignites spontaneously when it touches certain fuels — no spark needed, which is perfect for space.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — dinitrogen tetroxide is a hypergolic rocket propellant that ignites on contact with fuel. Extremely toxic and explosive!

compounds.realWorldUse: Yellow pigment, making tungsten metal for lightbulb filaments
compounds.funFact: Tungsten has the highest melting point of all metals at 3422°C. That's why lightbulb filaments are made from tungsten — they glow white-hot without melting!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making nickel metal, colouring glass green, fuel cells
compounds.funFact: The coin in your pocket probably contains nickel oxide on its surface — coins oxidise and the green-grey tarnish is partly nickel compounds. Nickel allergy is common!

compounds.realWorldUse: Humidity indicator (weather prediction cards), blue pigment
compounds.funFact: Cobalt chloride changes from blue when dry to pink when wet — it's used in weather prediction cards and 'magic' ink that appears and disappears! Amazing colour chemistry.

compounds.realWorldUse: Stomach medicine (Pepto-Bismol!), yellow pigment, fire safety
compounds.funFact: Pepto-Bismol contains bismuth compounds! Bismuth crystals form spectacular rainbow-coloured geometric structures — they're one of the most beautiful crystals in chemistry.

compounds.realWorldUse: Making sulfuric acid, vanadium redox flow batteries
compounds.funFact: Vanadium pentoxide is the key catalyst in making sulfuric acid — the world's most produced industrial chemical. Large-scale vanadium batteries can store city-scale energy!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — vanadium pentoxide is highly toxic and can cause severe lung damage and kidney failure!

compounds.realWorldUse: Washing powder, making glass, softening hard water
compounds.funFact: Ancient Egyptians extracted sodium carbonate from the ashes of burnt seaweed to make glass and soap. It's still one of the most used industrial chemicals today!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making glass, soap, cocoa processing, wine stabiliser
compounds.funFact: Potash was made by burning wood and leaching the ashes with water — that's why it's called POTash! It was so valuable it was one of North America's first exports.

compounds.realWorldUse: Mordant for dyeing fabric, etching metal
compounds.funFact: Iron nitrate is used as a mordant — it fixes dyes permanently to fabric. Before modern chemistry, natural dyes were unreliable without mordants like this.

compounds.realWorldUse: Fertiliser, concrete setting accelerator, wastewater treatment
compounds.funFact: Calcium nitrate is called 'Norwegian saltpetre' because it was first made in Norway using electricity from hydroelectric power — an early example of green chemistry!

compounds.realWorldUse: Cola drinks (gives the tangy taste!), rust removal, fertiliser
compounds.funFact: That tangy acidic taste in Coca-Cola comes from phosphoric acid! It also makes cola an effective rust remover — it reacts with iron oxide and dissolves it.

compounds.realWorldUse: Vinegar, making plastics and fibres, food preservation
compounds.funFact: Vinegar is just diluted acetic acid made by bacteria fermenting wine or cider. Concentrated acetic acid (glacial acetic acid) is actually quite dangerous!

compounds.realWorldUse: Food preservation, making biodegradable plastics, skincare
compounds.funFact: Lactic acid builds up in your muscles during intense exercise and causes the burning sensation. Sour milk, yogurt, and sourdough get their tang from lactic acid bacteria!

compounds.realWorldUse: Fertiliser (50% of world's nitrogen fertiliser!), skincare, de-icer
compounds.funFact: Urea was the first organic compound synthesised in a lab (1828), proving that 'life chemicals' can be made artificially. It's found naturally in urine!

compounds.realWorldUse: Food flavouring (E330), cleaning agent, bath bombs!
compounds.funFact: Lemons are about 8% citric acid by weight — that's what makes them sour! Bath bombs fizz because citric acid reacts with baking soda when wet to release CO₂.

compounds.realWorldUse: Your bones and teeth are mainly calcium phosphate!
compounds.funFact: Your skeleton is calcium phosphate! Bones are about 70% calcium phosphate mineral. Hydroxyapatite (a form of calcium phosphate) is also what makes teeth hard.

compounds.realWorldUse: Cleaning agent, food additive, water softener
compounds.funFact: Sodium phosphate is a strong cleaning agent used to degrease surfaces before painting. It's added to processed cheese to stop the oils separating — that's why American cheese melts so smoothly!

compounds.realWorldUse: Red and brown pigments, iron ore for making steel, polishing compound
compounds.funFact: Haematite has been used as red face paint for over 160,000 years — the earliest evidence of humans using cosmetics! Cave paintings use iron oxide pigments too.

compounds.realWorldUse: Chrome plating metals, cleaning laboratory glassware
compounds.funFact: Chromic acid is carcinogenic — it causes cancer. It was widely used to clean laboratory equipment but is being phased out due to its extreme toxicity!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — chromic acid is a carcinogen and severe oxidising agent that causes cancer and burns!

compounds.realWorldUse: Used historically as antiseptic and to treat syphilis (very dangerously!)
compounds.funFact: Mercuric chloride was given to syphilis patients in the 1800s. The treatment was often as deadly as the disease — 'a night with Venus, a lifetime with Mercury' was the saying!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — mercuric chloride is one of the most toxic mercury compounds, causing kidney failure and death!

compounds.realWorldUse: Used historically as rat poison; modern use in treating some leukaemias
compounds.funFact: Arsenic trioxide was the favourite poison of the Borgias in Renaissance Italy! Incredibly, it's now used in tiny doses to treat a specific type of leukaemia — poison as medicine!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — arsenic trioxide is a deadly poison historically used for murder. Even tiny amounts can kill!

compounds.realWorldUse: Antiseptic eyewash, ant killer, fire retardant, making borosilicate glass
compounds.funFact: Pyrex glass (used for oven dishes and lab equipment) contains boric acid. It's why it doesn't crack when going from hot to cold — boron changes the glass structure!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making mirrors (first used to silver mirrors), food preservative, reducing agent
compounds.funFact: The first proper mirrors were made by coating glass with tin chloride to deposit a thin layer of silver. Before this, people used polished metal or still water to see their reflection!

compounds.realWorldUse: Inside car batteries (lead-acid batteries)
compounds.funFact: Lead sulfate forms on the plates of car batteries during discharge. Recharging the battery converts it back to lead and sulfuric acid — a reversible chemical reaction!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — lead sulfate is a toxic lead compound that accumulates in the body and causes brain damage!

compounds.realWorldUse: Fireproofing wood, preserving eggs, making silica gel
compounds.funFact: Sodium silicate solution is called 'water glass' and was used to preserve eggs for months before refrigerators! Drop it in acid and it forms beautiful chemical garden structures.

compounds.realWorldUse: Photography, leather tanning, pyrotechnics (orange colour)
compounds.funFact: Potassium dichromate makes brilliant orange flames and was used in early photography. The 'chemical volcano' demonstration uses it — it puffs up into a giant wormy mass!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — potassium dichromate is a carcinogen and powerful oxidiser. It causes cancer and severe chemical burns!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making cement, treating soil acidity, water purification
compounds.funFact: Quicklime heated with intense flame glows with brilliant white light — this was used in theatre spotlights before electric lights, giving us the phrase 'in the limelight'!

compounds.realWorldUse: Oxygen source in submarines and breathing apparatus, bleaching
compounds.funFact: Sodium peroxide is used in submarine emergency breathing systems! When water or CO₂ touches it, it releases pure oxygen — brilliant for emergencies but very reactive!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — sodium peroxide reacts violently and explosively with water, organic materials and most other substances!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making plastics and pharmaceuticals (in industry, carefully)
compounds.funFact: HCN smells like almonds and acts within seconds — it stops cells using oxygen, causing almost instant unconsciousness. Apple seeds contain small amounts of a compound that releases it!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — hydrogen cyanide is one of the most lethal poisons known. Even tiny amounts cause almost instant death!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making viscose rayon fabric, solvent for rubber
compounds.funFact: Carbon disulfide ignites at only 90°C — well below boiling water temperature. It can catch fire just from a hot lightbulb! Workers who use it regularly can suffer nerve damage.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — carbon disulfide is extremely flammable (ignites at 90°C) and neurotoxic. It can catch fire from warm surfaces!

compounds.realWorldUse: Cutting tools, sandpaper, bulletproof vests, electric car components
compounds.funFact: Silicon carbide is almost as hard as diamond! Naturally occurring SiC (called moissanite) was first found in a meteor crater. Lab-grown moissanite is used in jewellery.

compounds.realWorldUse: Lubricant, cosmetics, bulletproof materials, semiconductor
compounds.funFact: Cubic boron nitride is the second hardest material after diamond! It's used to make cutting tools for machining steel. Hexagonal boron nitride is slippery like graphite.

compounds.realWorldUse: Making acetylene for welding, ripening fruit artificially
compounds.funFact: Unscrupulous fruit sellers use calcium carbide to artificially ripen mangoes and bananas! It reacts with moisture to make acetylene gas, which triggers ripening hormones.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — calcium carbide reacts with water to produce explosive acetylene gas!

compounds.realWorldUse: Emergency breathing apparatus for miners and firefighters
compounds.funFact: Potassium superoxide reacts with the CO₂ and water you breathe out to regenerate fresh oxygen — making it perfect for breathing apparatus in fires and mines!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — potassium superoxide reacts explosively with water and most organic materials!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making special alloys and ceramics, catalyst
compounds.funFact: When magnesium burns in air, it makes both magnesium oxide AND magnesium nitride. The nitride reacts with water to make ammonia — that distinctive sharp smell!

compounds.realWorldUse: Purifying drinking water, making paper, dyeing fabric, antiperspirant
compounds.funFact: Aluminium sulfate makes drinking water safe! It clumps dirt particles together so they sink to the bottom — a process called flocculation used in every water treatment plant.

compounds.realWorldUse: Purifying nickel metal (Mond process)
compounds.funFact: Nickel carbonyl is liquid at room temperature and is among the most toxic substances known — more toxic than carbon monoxide. It's used to make ultra-pure nickel for electronics!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — nickel carbonyl is one of the most toxic substances known and causes lung and brain damage from tiny exposures!

compounds.realWorldUse: Cleaning semiconductor manufacturing chambers, making LCD screens
compounds.funFact: NF₃ is a powerful greenhouse gas — 17,000 times more potent than CO₂! Ironically it's used to make 'green' solar panels and LCD screens. Its environmental impact is controversial.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — nitrogen trifluoride is a powerful oxidiser and toxic gas that attacks the nervous system!

compounds.realWorldUse: Electrical insulation in power stations, making aluminium, eye surgery
compounds.funFact: SF₆ makes your voice go very deep — the opposite of helium! It's 5x denser than air so it sinks and you can float a paper boat on invisible SF₆. The world's most potent greenhouse gas!

compounds.realWorldUse: Etching silicon chips, making semiconductors
compounds.funFact: Xenon was thought to be completely unreactive — noble gases don't react, right? Wrong! XeF₂ proved chemistry wrong in 1962 and shocked the scientific world!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — xenon difluoride is a powerful fluorinating agent that reacts explosively with water and organic materials!

compounds.realWorldUse: Photography, blueprint printing, food colouring, wine making
compounds.funFact: Blueprints — technical drawings with white lines on blue — were made using potassium ferricyanide! The photosensitive process was replaced by digital printing only in the 1990s.

compounds.realWorldUse: Making beautiful patina on copper and bronze artworks, pyrotechnics
compounds.funFact: Copper nitrate is used to artificially age bronze sculptures — it creates the green patina that usually takes decades to form naturally. Museum conservators use it deliberately!

compounds.realWorldUse: Green fireworks and signal flares, rat poison
compounds.funFact: The brightest green colour in fireworks comes from barium nitrate. Military signal flares and tracer ammunition use it. It's also highly toxic to mammals!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — barium nitrate is both a toxic heavy metal compound and an explosive oxidiser used in fireworks and ammunition!

compounds.realWorldUse: Advanced batteries, hydrogen storage for fuel cells
compounds.funFact: Lithium is the only metal that reacts with nitrogen gas at room temperature! This makes lithium unique — all other metals need special conditions to form nitrides.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — lithium nitride reacts explosively with water and is highly caustic!

compounds.realWorldUse: Gold mining (dissolves gold from rock), photography, electroplating
compounds.funFact: KCN dissolves gold — that's how most gold is extracted from rock! Cyanide leaching is highly controversial due to environmental damage when the waste escapes into rivers.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — potassium cyanide is a lethal poison that kills within minutes. It has been used for executions and in war crimes!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making insecticides, flame retardants and medicines
compounds.funFact: PCl₃ was used to make nerve agents in WWII. It reacts violently with water, producing HCl gas and phosphorous acid. Even moisture in air makes it fume!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — phosphorus trichloride reacts explosively with water and is used in making chemical weapons!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making sulfuric acid (industrial process)
compounds.funFact: SO₃ reacts so violently with water that adding it directly causes explosions of boiling acid. Industrial plants dissolve it in concentrated sulfuric acid first to be safe!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — sulfur trioxide reacts explosively with water to form concentrated sulfuric acid!

compounds.realWorldUse: Rocket fuel, making semiconductors, chemical synthesis
compounds.funFact: Diborane was seriously considered as rocket fuel for nuclear-powered aircraft in the 1950s! It ignites spontaneously in air and burns with an eerie green flame.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — diborane ignites spontaneously in air and is highly toxic. It was developed as a potential chemical weapon!

compounds.realWorldUse: Research chemical, oxygen atom source for making other compounds
compounds.funFact: XeF₄ was the second noble gas compound ever made — just weeks after XeF₂ in 1962. It's a powerful fluorinating agent and reacts explosively with water!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — xenon tetrafluoride reacts explosively with water producing ozone and hydrogen fluoride!

compounds.realWorldUse: Copper ore (chalcocite) — primary source of copper metal
compounds.funFact: Most of the world's copper comes from chalcocite (copper sulfide) ores! Smelting separates the copper and sulfur using heat — the sulfur becomes SO₂ gas.

compounds.realWorldUse: The hard component of steel — makes it strong and tough
compounds.funFact: Steel is iron with a tiny amount of carbon (0.2-2.1%). The carbon forms iron carbide crystals that make steel much stronger than pure iron. Without it we couldn't build bridges!

compounds.realWorldUse: Photographic film, photochromic glasses, electrodes
compounds.funFact: Old photographic film contains silver chloride — it darkens when light hits it, recording the image. Digital cameras made silver film mostly obsolete after 150 years of use!

compounds.realWorldUse: Electroplating with gold, toning photographs, making ruby glass
compounds.funFact: Medieval glassmakers added tiny amounts of gold chloride to glass to make stunning ruby red stained glass — the gold particles are so small they scatter light to appear red!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making cisplatin cancer drug, catalyst for fuel cells
compounds.funFact: Cisplatin, derived from platinum chloride, is one of the most successful cancer drugs ever made — it's used to treat testicular cancer with a 99% cure rate!
